did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses Deep Convolutional Neural Networks (DCNNs), specifically VGG-16 architectures, to perform automatic classification of skin tumours (malignant melanomas vs. benign lesions) using dermoscopy images from the HAM10000 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and testing of a deep convolutional neural network (DCNN) system for automatic classification of skin tumours. It details specific methods (VGG-16 architectures, data augmentation), a specific dataset (HAM10000), and reports new quantitative results (accuracy metrics between 96.5% and 97.5%) compared to baseline approaches, which characterizes it as an original research study rather than a review.

title: Deep Convolutional Neural Networks on Automatic Classification for Skin Tumour Images
journal: Journal of Image and Graphics Processing
brief_summary: This study aimed to automatically classify skin tumours as malignant melanomas or benign lesions using dermoscopy images. The researchers employed Deep Convolutional Neural Networks (DCNNs), specifically utilizing the VGG-16 architecture, to perform this binary classification task. The model was trained and tested on a subset of the HAM10000 dataset, which included 2,134 original images and 10,760 augmented images. The evaluation approach involved comparing three VGG-16 configurations: one without augmentation, one with input augmentation, and one with modified fully connected layers. The key result showed that the proposed system achieved mean accuracy values ranging from 96.48% to 97.33%, with the augmented approach yielding the highest performance. These findings suggest that the DCNN-based strategy is effective for automatic skin tumour classification.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study focuses on a binary classification task distinguishing malignant melanomas from benign lesions. The dataset subset used contained 1,163 cases of malignant skin tumours (specifically melanomas as stated in the aim and conclusion) and 971 cases of benign skin tumours.
Used as the comparator class in the binary classification task. The subset of the HAM10000 dataset used for the experiment included 971 cases of benign skin tumours.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin tumours into malignant melanoma or benign lesion categories
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the aim is the 'classification of skin tumours' and focuses on a 'binary classification of benign and malignant changes'. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (malignant melanoma vs. benign lesions) using dermoscopy images from the HAM10000 dataset. Performance is measured using accuracy metrics (mean accuracy between 96.48% and 97.33%).

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A publicly available repository of dermatoscopic images from different populations, acquired and stored by different modalities. The full dataset contains 10,015 images across seven classes. For this study, a subset was selected containing 2,134 original images (971 benign skin tumours and 1,163 malignant skin tumours/melanomas) after excluding images with vignettes.

use.training: The 2,134 original images were used as the base input. Additionally, 10,760 augmented images were generated from this subset (totaling 12,903 images) for training the VGG-16 models with data augmentation techniques (rotation, zoom, shear, flip).
use.testing: The model performance was evaluated on the dataset, reporting mean accuracy metrics (96.48% to 97.33%) across different VGG-16 configurations.
use.other: The full HAM10000 dataset (10,015 instances) was referenced for context and class distribution (Table 1), but only the specific binary subset was used for the experimental classification task.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the performance of the classification system by calculating the probability that the diagnostic test is performed correctly.
To summarize the performance distribution across five repeated trainings and evaluations for three different network approaches (PS, PS + AUG, ALL).
To perform five trainings and evaluations to ensure the results are a representative sample of the three approaches.
To monitor training progress and detect overfitting by observing the change in the Loss function per epoch for training and test datasets.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive statistics (mean, minimum, and maximum accuracy) across five repeated trainings and evaluations.

HAM10000.dataset_description: Subset of HAM10000 containing 2,134 images (971 benign skin tumours, 1,163 malignant skin tumours/melanomas) after excluding images with vignettes. Split: 80% training (1,711 instances), 20% testing (428 instances).
HAM10000.evaluation_setting: Five repeated trainings and evaluations for three network approaches. Training conducted in mini-batch mode for 50 epochs.

PS.description: Modification of only the last layer without augmentation.

PS + AUG.description: Modification of the last layer with input data augmentation (rotation up to 20 degrees, zoom up to 15%, width/height change up to 20%, shear up to 15 degrees, horizontal flip). Input dataset size: 10,670 images.

ALL.description: Modification of fully connected layers (two layers with 128 neurons, last layer with 2 neurons). Parameters trained: 3,228,162 out of 17,942,850.

HAM10000.notes: The difference between the best and worst individual accuracy across approaches was 2.1%. The difference in mean accuracy between approaches was 0.84%. The approach with the last layer modified with augmentation achieved the best maximum result. Training with all fully connected layers showed improved accuracy compared to modifying only the last layer. Loss function graphs indicated slight overfitting starting after 20 epochs for the PS approach.

architecture_used.pretraining: Fine-tuned weights from ImageNet dataset (14,000 images divided into 1,000 classes)

architecture_details.base_structure: VGG-16 network architecture consisting of 16 layers divided into six segments. Segments contain convolution layers (first two segments have two convolution layers each, other segments have three convolution layers each). Last layer is max pooling. Three fully connected layers follow.

modifications.description: Modification of only the last layer without augmentation. All weighted parameters fixed in every layer except the last layer. Last layer contains 2 neurons. 8,194 parameters trained out of 138,268,738 total.

Modification of the last layer with input data augmentation. Input dataset size: 10,670 images. Augmentation techniques: rotation up to 20 degrees, zoom up to 15%, width/height change up to 20%, shear up to 15 degrees, horizontal flip.

Modification of all fully connected layers. Two layers have 128 neurons, last layer has 2 neurons. Values of network parameter values learning in the last three completely connected layers. 3,228,162 parameters trained out of 17,942,850 total.

architecture_details.output_layer: Last layer contains 2 neurons with softmax activation function for binary classification (malignant melanoma vs. benign lesions).

training_details.loss_function: Not explicitly named, but monitored via 'Loss' function graphs per epoch to detect overfitting.

classification_or_output_head.type: Fully connected layer with softmax activation
classification_or_output_head.purpose: Binary classification of skin tumours (malignant melanoma vs. benign lesions)

preprocessing_techniques: Exclusion of images with vignettes (shading off gradually into the surrounding photograph) to prevent asymmetry and recognition difficulties.
Data augmentation applied to the input dataset before training, including: rotation up to 20 degrees, zoom up to 15%, width and height change up to 20%, shear up to 15 degrees, and horizontal flip.
Image resizing to 224 × 224 RGB pixels for the VGG-16 input layer.
conclusions: The proposed deep convolutional neural network (DCNN) strategy based on the VGG-16 architecture is effective for the automatic binary classification of skin tumours (malignant melanomas vs. benign lesions) using dermoscopy images.
The system achieved high mean accuracy values ranging from 96.48% to 97.33% across three tested VGG-16 configurations on the HAM10000 dataset subset.

Modifying all fully connected layers (ALL approach) yielded the highest mean accuracy (97.33%) and maximum accuracy (97.89%), outperforming approaches that modified only the last layer (PS and PS + AUG).

Data augmentation techniques (rotation, zoom, shear, flip) improved the maximum accuracy result but did not significantly improve the mean accuracy compared to the non-augmented approach, with a mean difference of only 0.84%.
The difference between the best and worst individual accuracy across the three approaches was 2.1%, indicating that the measured performance for all approaches was very similar.

Training for 50 epochs was necessary as accuracy stabilized relatively quickly; training for fewer epochs (e.g., 30) was insufficient, while further training risked causing overfitting, evidenced by rising loss functions after 20 epochs.

The study acknowledges that while the system is effective, future work should include broadening the dataset with new classes and creating new DL models combined with novel AI techniques to solve real-world skin tumour data sets.
important_remarks: The study is limited to a binary classification task (malignant melanoma vs. benign lesions) rather than the full seven-class HAM10000 dataset, potentially limiting generalizability to other skin tumour types.
The dataset used is a subset of HAM10000 with images excluded due to vignettes, which may introduce selection bias regarding image quality and lesion visibility.
The paper does not report formal statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values); performance differences between models are described using descriptive statistics (mean, min, max accuracy) only.

Data augmentation was applied only to the training set for one model configuration (PS + AUG), creating an imbalance in input data volume (10,670 augmented images vs. 2,134 original images) which may confound direct comparison of model architecture efficacy versus data volume effects.
The study acknowledges potential overfitting, noting that loss functions indicated slight overfitting starting after 20 epochs, yet training was conducted for 50 epochs.
The dataset lacks clinical dimensions (e.g., mole size) and metadata beyond image classes, limiting the ability to correlate model performance with clinical features.

The research is based on a single dataset (HAM10000 subset) without external validation on independent cohorts, raising concerns about external validity and generalizability to different populations or imaging modalities.

The paper notes that smartphone applications for skin cancer detection carry a risk of missing melanomas if they reassure users incorrectly, highlighting a critical clinical safety concern for deployment.
The study is an extension of previous work by the same authors, which may introduce continuity bias or lack of independent replication.
